title: About
text:
I am Jun Ha Chang, PhD, a researcher working with real-world data, electronic health records, wearable sensor data, and digital biomarkers in neurodegenerative disorders.
This site includes practical notes on EHR data quality, real-world data, real-world evidence, and reproducible R examples.
Views expressed here are my own.
All examples are synthetic toy data created for educational purposes. They do not contain real patient records, patient identifiers, institutional data, or results from any specific health system. They are informed by common data patterns encountered in EHR/RWD work, but they do not represent any actual patient, clinical case, or health system.

title: Cohort Feasibility Assessment Using Synthetic EHR Data
text:
Even before you start analyzing your data and think about model or even start grant application, we need to first check whether we have enough data to do that.
In this post, suppose that we want to test feasibilty of our thought (association between sleepd disorders and incident Alzhdimer’s disease risk). The goal of this post is to test feasibilty: how many patients meet basic cohort criteria, and what are their simple demographics?
For this example, I used three synthetic EHR tables: patients, diagnoses, and encounters. The data are fully synthetic and are intended to mimic common relational EHR structures. You can create your own using R code that I shared and found in : … (add path or repositories)
The final output will be:

A cohort attrition table
A simple demographic summary of the final feasible cohort.



First of all, we need to define our cohort. For this example, we will define our cohort as follows:

Has at least one sleep disorder diagnosis.
Index date is the first sleep disorder diagnosis date.
Age is at least 20 years old at index date.
Has no Alzheimer’s disease diagnosis before or on the index date.
Has at least 365 days of observed follow-up after index date.

This is the minimum criteria for our cohort. In practice, you may have more complex criteria, but the logic will be similar.





Code
# Libraries for this project, you can ignore as it is mostly for data manipulation and database connection in R environments. -----
library(DBI) # connect to databases and send data or queries to them.
library(RSQLite) # SQLite database driver for R
library(dplyr) # data manipulation and analysis
library(readr) # read CSV files into R

con <- dbConnect(SQLite(), ":memory:") # create an in-memory SQLite database for demonstration purposes. In practice, you would connect to your actual database.

# I save the synthetic data to CSV files for demonstration purposes. In practice, you would load your actual EHR data from your database or other sources.
patients <- read_csv("data/synthetic_patients.csv") %>%
  mutate(BIRTH_DATE = as.character(BIRTH_DATE),
         DEATH_DATE = as.character(DEATH_DATE))
diagnoses <- read_csv("data/synthetic_diagnoses.csv") %>%
  mutate(DX_DATE = as.character(DX_DATE))
encounters <- read_csv("data/synthetic_encounters.csv") %>%
  mutate(ENCOUNTER_DATE = as.character(ENCOUNTER_DATE))

dbWriteTable(con, "patients", patients, overwrite = TRUE)
dbWriteTable(con, "diagnoses", diagnoses, overwrite = TRUE)
dbWriteTable(con, "encounters", encounters, overwrite = TRUE)


This creates a temporary SQLite database in memory and loads the 3 synthetic EHR tables into it.



This project uses three synthetic EHR-style tables. The important point is that the tables do not have the same unit of analysis.









Table
Unit of analysis
Key columns
Role in feasibility test




patients
One row per patient
PATID, BIRTH_DATE, DEATH_DATE, SEX, RACE
Defines baseline demographics and death date


diagnoses
Multiple rows per patient
PATID, ICD10, ICD10_TXT, DX_DATE
Defines sleep disorder index date and Alzheimer’s disease diagnosis


encounters
Multiple rows per patient
PATID, ENCOUNTER_ID, ENCOUNTER_DATE, ENCOUNTER_TYPE
Defines EHR activity, baseline lookback, and follow-up



The three tables are linked by PATID.





erDiagram
    patients ||--o{ diagnoses : "PATID"
    patients ||--o{ encounters : "PATID"

    patients {
        string PATID
        date BIRTH_DATE
        date DEATH_DATE
        string SEX
        string RACE
    }

    diagnoses {
        string PATID
        string ICD10
        string ICD10_TXT
        date DX_DATE
    }

    encounters {
        string PATID
        string ENCOUNTER_ID
        date ENCOUNTER_DATE
        string ENCOUNTER_TYPE
    }






This structure matters because joining diagnosis or encounter records directly to the patient table can create duplicate patient rows. For feasibility assessment, the final output must return to one row per patient.
Let’s quickly check first three rows of each data structure.


Code
SELECT *
FROM patients
LIMIT 3;



3 records


PATID
BIRTH_DATE
DEATH_DATE
SEX
RACE

1952-07-21
NA
Female
White


P0002
1976-07-10
NA
Male
White


P0003
1959-01-31
NA
Female
Black







Code
SELECT *
FROM diagnoses
LIMIT 3;



3 records








PATID
ICD10
ICD10_TXT
DX_DATE




P0001
E78.5
Hyperlipidemia, unspecified
2018-08-08


P0001
I25.10
Atherosclerotic heart disease of native coronary artery without angina pectoris
2023-08-09


P0001
G47.9
Sleep disorder, unspecified
2024-12-02







Code
SELECT *
FROM encounters
LIMIT 3;



3 records


PATID
ENCOUNTER_DATE
ENCOUNTER_TYPE
ENCOUNTER_ID

2016-04-09
Primary care
E001


P0001
2018-12-23
Outpatient

P0001
2020-03-08
Outpatient
E003








Before defining the cohort, first thing to do is check the basic size of each table (number of unique patients)


Code
SELECT COUNT(*) AS n_patients
FROM patients;

SELECT COUNT(*) AS n_diagnoses
       COUNT(DISTINCT PATID) AS n_unique_patients
FROM diagnoses;

SELECT COUNT(*)



1 records


n_patients




100

Code

Before defining the cohort, first thing to do is check the basic size of each table (number of unique patients)


Code
SELECT COUNT(*) AS n_patients
FROM patients;

title: Duplicate Records in EHR Data
text:
Duplicate records are one of the first things I check in EHR data because they can quietly distort almost every downstream analysis. But, the tricky part is that not all repeated rows mean the same thing.
Some are true data errors. Some are repeated documentation. Some reflect different clinical or administrative sources representing related information. In other words, repeated rows are not always wrong, but unexplained repeated rows are dangerous.
This is easy to overlook. At first glance, the data may look fine: same patient, same date, same diagnosis, multiple rows. It is tempting to just remove duplicates with distinct() or unique() and then move on. But in EHR data, deduplication is not just an automated coding step. It is a clinical and analytic decision.
Before removing rows, I usually ask a simple question:
What is one row supposed to represent?

A patient?
A diagnosis code?
A unique medication?

A pharmacy dispensing record?

The answer changes what should be kept or removed.
There are many other situations, but in this post, I walk through the three common situations where repeated rows appear in EHR data and explain why they should not all be handled the same way.



My rough planning note I made before structuring an EHR dataset.

title: Duplicate Records in EHR Data
text:
Duplicate records are one of the first things I check in EHR data because they can quietly distort almost every downstream analysis. But, the tricky part is that not all repeated rows mean the same thing.
Some are true data errors. Some are repeated documentation. Some reflect different clinical or administrative sources representing related information. In other words, repeated rows are not always wrong, but unexplained repeated rows are dangerous.
This is easy to overlook. At first glance, the data may look fine: same patient, same date, same diagnosis, multiple rows. It is tempting to just remove duplicates with distinct() or unique() and then move on. But in EHR data, deduplication is not just an automated coding step. It is a clinical and analytic decision.
Before removing rows, I usually ask a simple question:
What is one row supposed to represent?

A patient?
A diagnosis code?
A unique medication?

A pharmacy dispensing record?

The answer changes what should be kept or removed.
There are many other situations, but in this post, I walk through the three common situations where repeated rows appear in EHR data and explain why they should not all be handled the same way.



My rough planning note I made before structuring an EHR dataset.

title: Duplicate Records in EHR Data
section: Example 1. Related diagnosis codes on the same date
text:
Example 1. Related diagnosis codes on the same date
One common situation I see is multiple diagnosis codes for the same patient on the same date. At first glance, this looks like a duplicate problem. Sometimes, it is. But often, it is more complicated than that.
For example, if we are defining a sleep disorder cohort using ICD-10 codes, the same patient may have several sleep-related codes recorded on the same date. These codes may reflect related diagnoses, specific conditions, or non-specific documentation within the broader sleep-disorder category.
The problem is not simply that there are multiple rows. The problem is whether we know what those rows mean for the question we are asking.

Toy data example
Suppose we extracted diagnosis records for patients with sleep disorders using G47.xx codes. The following table shows a small subset of the data:

ehr_dx <- tibble::tribble( ~PATID, ~DATE_DX, ~ICD10, ~ICD10_TXT, 
                           "AA001", "2026-01-15", "G47.00", "Insomnia, unspecified", 
                           "AA001", "2026-01-15", "G47.33", "Obstructive sleep apnea", 
                           "AA001", "2026-01-15", "G47.9", "Sleep disorder, unspecified", 
                           "BB002", "2026-02-10", "G47.00", "Insomnia, unspecified", 
                           "CC003", "2026-03-05", "G47.33", "Obstructive sleep apnea" )

ehr_dx

# A tibble: 5 × 4
  PATID DATE_DX    ICD10  ICD10_TXT                  
  <chr> <chr>      <chr>  <chr>                      
1 AA001 2026-01-15 G47.00 Insomnia, unspecified      
2 AA001 2026-01-15 G47.33 Obstructive sleep apnea    
3 AA001 2026-01-15 G47.9  Sleep disorder, unspecified
4 BB002 2026-02-10 G47.00 Insomnia, unspecified      
5 CC003 2026-03-05 G47.33 Obstructive sleep apnea    


In this toy dataset, individual “AA001” has three sleep-related diagnosis codes on the same date. Whether this is a problem depends on our question.
If the goal is to identify whether an individual has any sleep disorder, “AA001” should likely be counted once. If the goal is to examine specific sleep-related diagnosis and clinical complexity, these codes should be maintained because AA001 has insomnia (G47.00), OSA (G47.33), and unspecified sleep disorders (G47.9).
This is why I try not to “clean” these rows too quickly. Cleaning without defining the target structure can remove information that may be useful later.


Why it matters
If we count each diagnosis code as a separate patient or event, we may overestimate the prevalence of sleep disorders. But, if we collapse all sleep-related codes too early, we may lose clinically useful information about specific conditions (e.g., subtypes).

nrow(ehr_dx)

[1] 5


There are five diagnosis rows. But if we count unique patients, there are only three patients:

n_distinct(ehr_dx$PATID)

[1] 3


This difference matters. If we confuse diagnosis rows with patients, we may overestimate disease burden, exposure frequency, or clinical complexity.


How to check
A simple first check is to compare the number of rows with the number of unique patients.

ehr_dx %>%
  summarise(
    n_rows = n(), 
    n_unique_patients = n_distinct(PATID)
  )

# A tibble: 1 × 2
  n_rows n_unique_patients
   <int>             <int>
1      5                 3


Next, check how many diagnosis codes each patient has on each date.

ehr_dx %>%
  count(PATID, DATE_DX, name = "n_diagnosis_codes") %>%
  filter(n_diagnosis_codes > 1)

# A tibble: 1 × 3
  PATID DATE_DX    n_diagnosis_codes
  <chr> <chr>                  <int>
1 AA001 2026-01-15                 3


If the goal is to create a patient-level sleep disorder cohort, we can collapse the data to one row per patient.

sleep_cohort_patient_level <- ehr_dx %>%
  distinct(PATID, .keep_all = TRUE)

sleep_cohort_patient_level

# A tibble: 3 × 4
  PATID DATE_DX    ICD10  ICD10_TXT              
  <chr> <chr>      <chr>  <chr>                  
1 AA001 2026-01-15 G47.00 Insomnia, unspecified  
2 BB002 2026-02-10 G47.00 Insomnia, unspecified  
3 CC003 2026-03-05 G47.33 Obstructive sleep apnea


However, I usually prefer keeping diagnosis-code information separately for later use. Reviewers, collaborators, or stakeholders may ask which specific diagnoses were included. Also, I may not need the subtype information today, but I often regret not keeping it later.
One way to keep this information is to summarize the unique diagnosis codes for each patient.

ehr_dx %>%
  group_by(PATID) %>%
  summarise(
    n_unique_dx_codes = n_distinct(ICD10),
    dx_codes = paste(unique(ICD10), collapse = " | "),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# A tibble: 3 × 3
  PATID n_unique_dx_codes dx_codes               
  <chr>             <int> <chr>                  
1 AA001                 3 G47.00 | G47.33 | G47.9
2 BB002                 1 G47.00                 
3 CC003                 1 G47.33                 


Tip: Once you define structure, collapse for the analysis, but keep the information somewhere.

title: Duplicate Records in EHR Data
text:
Example 2. Merge-created duplicates
In EHR data, we often combine information from multiple sources: billing records, encounter diagnoses, clinician visit records, medication orders, pharmacy dispensing records, and problem lists. When these sources are merged, the same clinical episode may appear more than once for the same patient.
Suppose we want to identify patients who were newly diagnosed with insomnia, prescribed zolpidem, and actually picked up the medication from the pharmacy. This sounds simple, but it requires linking at least three sources: diagnosis records, medication orders, and pharmacy dispensing records.
The challenge is that each source has a different meaning:

a diagnosis record indicates that insomnia was documented;
a medication order indicates that zolpidem was prescribed;
a pharmacy dispensing record indicates that the medication was actually picked up or dispensed.

If we merge these sources without defining the analytic unit, one clinical episode can easily turn into multiple rows.

Toy data example

diagnosis_data <- tribble(
  ~PATID,  ~DATE_DX, ~ENCOUNTER_ID, ~ICD10, ~ICD10_TXT,
  "AA001", "2026-01-15",    "E1001",      "G47.00",        "Insomnia, unspecified",
  "BB002", "2026-02-10",    "E1002",      "G47.00",        "Insomnia, unspecified",
  "CC003", "2026-03-05",    "E1003",      "G47.00",        "Insomnia, unspecified"
)

medication_order_data <- tribble(
  ~PATID,  ~DATE_ORDER,  ~ENCOUNTER_ID, ~ATC_CODE, ~RX_NAME, ~DOSE, ~DAYS_SUPPLY_ORDERED,
  "AA001", "2026-01-15", "E1001", "N05CF02",  "zolpidem",  "5 mg", 30,
  "BB002", "2026-02-10", "E1002", "N05CF02",  "zolpidem",  "5 mg", 30,
  "CC003", "2026-03-05", "E1003", "N05CF02",  "zolpidem",  "5 mg", 30
)

pharmacy_dispensing_data <- tribble(
  ~PATID,  ~DATE_DISPENSE, ~RX_NAME, ~ATC_CODE,  ~DOSE,    ~DAYS_SUPPLY_DISPENSED,
  "AA001", "2026-01-17",   "zolpidem",       "N05CF02", "5 mg",   30,
  "AA001", "2026-01-17",   "atorvastatin",   "C10AA05", "20 mg",  30,
  "AA001", "2026-01-17",   "metformin",      "A10BA02", "500 mg", 30,
  "BB002", "2026-02-10",   "zolpidem",       "N05CF02", "5 mg",   30
  # CC003 had a medication order, but no dispensing record
)

Now suppose we merge the dispensing data by “PATID” only.

bad_merge <- diagnosis_data %>%
  left_join(medication_order_data, by = c("PATID", "ENCOUNTER_ID")) %>%
  left_join(pharmacy_dispensing_data, by = "PATID")

bad_merge

# A tibble: 5 × 15
  PATID DATE_DX    ENCOUNTER_ID ICD10  ICD10_TXT DATE_ORDER ATC_CODE.x RX_NAME.x
  <chr> <chr>      <chr>        <chr>  <chr>     <chr>      <chr>      <chr>    
1 AA001 2026-01-15 E1001        G47.00 Insomnia… 2026-01-15 N05CF02    zolpidem 
2 AA001 2026-01-15 E1001        G47.00 Insomnia… 2026-01-15 N05CF02    zolpidem 
3 AA001 2026-01-15 E1001        G47.00 Insomnia… 2026-01-15 N05CF02    zolpidem 
4 BB002 2026-02-10 E1002        G47.00 Insomnia… 2026-02-10 N05CF02    zolpidem 
5 CC003 2026-03-05 E1003        G47.00 Insomnia… 2026-03-05 N05CF02    zolpidem 
# ℹ 7 more variables: DOSE.x <chr>, DAYS_SUPPLY_ORDERED <dbl>,
#   DATE_DISPENSE <chr>, RX_NAME.y <chr>, ATC_CODE.y <chr>, DOSE.y <chr>,
#   DAYS_SUPPLY_DISPENSED <dbl>


This creates repeated rows for AA001. The insomnia-zolpidem pair is now linked not only to zolpidem, but also to atorvastatin and metformin. The problem is not that AA001 has multiple medications. The problem is that the merge attached unrelated medications to the wrong clinical episode.


Why it matters
For the simple question, “Did this individual pick up zolpidem?”, the error may be easy to notice. But for medication exposure calculations, this can become a serious problem.
If we calculate total medication burden, days supplied, or number of medications after the insomnia diagnosis based on prescription data, a broad merge by PATID alone can create a misleading picture of medication exposure. In this example, AA001 would look like they had three medications attached to the insomnia-zolpidem episode, even though only zolpidem is relevant to the question.
This can lead to:

overcounting the number of prescribed medications linked to an encounter;

inflating total days supplied;

assigning unrelated medications to the wrong diagnosis;

misclassifying medication exposure;




How to check
First, check whether the merge increased the number of rows.

diagnosis_data %>%
  summarise(
    original_diagnosis_rows = n(),
    original_patients = n_distinct(PATID)
  )

# A tibble: 1 × 2
  original_diagnosis_rows original_patients
                    <int>             <int>
1                       3                 3

bad_merge %>%
  summarise(
    merged_rows = n(),
    merged_patients = n_distinct(PATID)
  )

# A tibble: 1 × 2
  merged_rows merged_patients
        <int>           <int>
1           5               3


If the row count increased, do not automatically remove duplicates. First check whether the increase is clinically meaningful or caused by an overly broad join.
For this case, we only need zolpidem dispensing records. One simple approach is to filter the dispensing data before merging.

pharmacy_dispensing_data_zolpidem <- pharmacy_dispensing_data %>%
  filter(RX_NAME == "zolpidem")

better_merge <- diagnosis_data %>%
  left_join(medication_order_data, by = c("PATID", "ENCOUNTER_ID")) %>%
  left_join(
    pharmacy_dispensing_data_zolpidem,
    by = c("PATID", "RX_NAME", "ATC_CODE", "DOSE"),
    suffix = c("_ORDER", "_DISPENSE")
  )

better_merge

# A tibble: 3 × 12
  PATID DATE_DX   ENCOUNTER_ID ICD10 ICD10_TXT DATE_ORDER ATC_CODE RX_NAME DOSE 
  <chr> <chr>     <chr>        <chr> <chr>     <chr>      <chr>    <chr>   <chr>
1 AA001 2026-01-… E1001        G47.… Insomnia… 2026-01-15 N05CF02  zolpid… 5 mg 
2 BB002 2026-02-… E1002        G47.… Insomnia… 2026-02-10 N05CF02  zolpid… 5 mg 
3 CC003 2026-03-… E1003        G47.… Insomnia… 2026-03-05 N05CF02  zolpid… 5 mg 
# ℹ 3 more variables: DAYS_SUPPLY_ORDERED <dbl>, DATE_DISPENSE <chr>,
#   DAYS_SUPPLY_DISPENSED <dbl>


Now we can answer the original question: who had an insomnia diagnosis, received a zolpidem order, and had evidence of pharmacy dispensing?

insomnia_zolpidem_dispensed <- better_merge %>% 
  filter(
    ICD10 == "G47.00", 
    RX_NAME == "zolpidem", 
    ATC_CODE == "N05CF02", 
    !is.na(DATE_DISPENSE)
    ) %>%
  select(PATID, ENCOUNTER_ID, DATE_DX, ICD10, RX_NAME, ATC_CODE, DATE_ORDER, DATE_DISPENSE, DOSE) 

insomnia_zolpidem_dispensed

# A tibble: 2 × 9
  PATID ENCOUNTER_ID DATE_DX    ICD10  RX_NAME ATC_CODE DATE_ORDER DATE_DISPENSE
  <chr> <chr>        <chr>      <chr>  <chr>   <chr>    <chr>      <chr>        
1 AA001 E1001        2026-01-15 G47.00 zolpid… N05CF02  2026-01-15 2026-01-17   
2 BB002 E1002        2026-02-10 G47.00 zolpid… N05CF02  2026-02-10 2026-02-10   
# ℹ 1 more variable: DOSE <chr>


In this toy example, AA001 and BB002 meet the full definition. Both had an insomnia diagnosis, a zolpidem order, and a matching pharmacy dispensing record. CC003 had an insomnia diagnosis and a zolpidem order, but no dispensing record, so CC003 should not be classified as having picked up zolpidem.
The important point is that the bad merge created repeated rows by linking all pharmacy records for AA001 to the insomnia-zolpidem episode. This is not a problem to fix with distinct(). The better solution is to fix the merge logic.
Tip: If a merge increases the numbber of rows, do not immediately deduplicate; first check whether the join keys were too broad.

title: Duplicate Records in EHR Data
text:
Example 3. Technical duplication
Some duplicate rows are true technical errors. (Unfortunately) Unlike the earlier examples, these rows do not reflect different diagnosis subtypes, different data sources, or a meaningful one-to-many relationship. They are simply repeated records.
This can happen during data extraction, SQL joins, repeated pulls, or file appending. This is the type of duplicate where distinct() may actually be appropriate, but only after confirming that the repeated rows are truly identical.

Toy data example

example3_data <- tribble(
  ~PATID,  ~DATE_DX,      ~ENCOUNTER_ID, ~ICD10,   ~RX_NAME,   ~ATC_CODE,  ~DOSE,
  "AA001", "2026-01-15", "E1001",       "G47.00", "zolpidem", "N05CF02", "5 mg",
  "AA001", "2026-01-15", "E1001",       "G47.00", "zolpidem", "N05CF02", "5 mg",
  "BB002", "2026-02-10", "E1002",       "G47.00", "zolpidem", "N05CF02", "5 mg",
  "CC003", "2026-03-05", "E1003",       "G47.00", "zolpidem", "N05CF02", "5 mg"
)

example3_data

# A tibble: 4 × 7
  PATID DATE_DX    ENCOUNTER_ID ICD10  RX_NAME  ATC_CODE DOSE 
  <chr> <chr>      <chr>        <chr>  <chr>    <chr>    <chr>
1 AA001 2026-01-15 E1001        G47.00 zolpidem N05CF02  5 mg 
2 AA001 2026-01-15 E1001        G47.00 zolpidem N05CF02  5 mg 
3 BB002 2026-02-10 E1002        G47.00 zolpidem N05CF02  5 mg 
4 CC003 2026-03-05 E1003        G47.00 zolpidem N05CF02  5 mg 


In this example, the first two rows are identical. They have the same patient ID, date, encounter ID, diagnosis code, medication name, ATC code, and dose. There is no additional clinical information in the second row.


Why it matters
Technical duplicates can inflate counts and distort downstream summaries.
For example, if we count the number of zolpidem-related records, AA001 is counted twice even though there is only one clinical episode.

example3_data %>%
  count(PATID, DATE_DX, ENCOUNTER_ID, ICD10, RX_NAME, ATC_CODE, DOSE, name = "n_rows")

# A tibble: 3 × 8
  PATID DATE_DX    ENCOUNTER_ID ICD10  RX_NAME  ATC_CODE DOSE  n_rows
  <chr> <chr>      <chr>        <chr>  <chr>    <chr>    <chr>  <int>
1 AA001 2026-01-15 E1001        G47.00 zolpidem N05CF02  5 mg       2
2 BB002 2026-02-10 E1002        G47.00 zolpidem N05CF02  5 mg       1
3 CC003 2026-03-05 E1003        G47.00 zolpidem N05CF02  5 mg       1


If we later calculate medication exposure, visit frequency, or diagnosis counts, this duplicated row may make the patient appear to have more records than they actually do.


How to check
A simple check is to count repeated rows across the variables that should define a unique record.

example3_data %>%
  count(PATID, DATE_DX, ENCOUNTER_ID, ICD10, RX_NAME, ATC_CODE, DOSE, name = "n_rows") %>%
  filter(n_rows > 1)

# A tibble: 1 × 8
  PATID DATE_DX    ENCOUNTER_ID ICD10  RX_NAME  ATC_CODE DOSE  n_rows
  <chr> <chr>      <chr>        <chr>  <chr>    <chr>    <chr>  <int>
1 AA001 2026-01-15 E1001        G47.00 zolpidem N05CF02  5 mg       2


If the duplicated rows are truly identical and do not represent separate clinical events, they can be removed.

example3_data_clean <- example3_data %>%
  distinct()

example3_data_clean

# A tibble: 3 × 7
  PATID DATE_DX    ENCOUNTER_ID ICD10  RX_NAME  ATC_CODE DOSE 
  <chr> <chr>      <chr>        <chr>  <chr>    <chr>    <chr>
1 AA001 2026-01-15 E1001        G47.00 zolpidem N05CF02  5 mg 
2 BB002 2026-02-10 E1002        G47.00 zolpidem N05CF02  5 mg 
3 CC003 2026-03-05 E1003        G47.00 zolpidem N05CF02  5 mg 


Then compare the number of rows before and after removing exact duplicates.

tibble(
  rows_before = nrow(example3_data),
  rows_after = nrow(example3_data_clean)
)

# A tibble: 1 × 2
  rows_before rows_after
        <int>      <int>
1           4          3


Tip: Use distinct() only after confirming that the repeated rows are truly identical.

title: Duplicate Records in EHR Data
text:
Final thought
Deduplication sounds like a simple preprocessing step. In practice, I have found that it is one of those small steps that people often rush through because the table looks “clean enough” at first glance.
That is where the risk starts.
Repeated rows in EHR data are not always errors. Some repeated rows reflect clinically meaningful information, some are created during merging, and some are true technical duplicates. Treating all of them the same way can quietly change the cohort, inflate exposure counts, or remove useful clinical information.
Before deduplicating, I usually ask three questions:

Do the repeated rows represent different clinical information?
Were the repeated rows created by the merge?
Are the rows truly identical technical duplicates?

The goal is not to make the dataset smaller. The goal is to make sure each row represents the intended analytic unit.
In EHR data, “small” preprocessing decisions can become large analytic errors later. I hope sharing these practical examples helps others who are just starting to work with EHR data avoid some of these mistakes earlier.
Note: All examples are synthetic toy data created for educational purposes. They do not contain real patient records, patient identifiers, institutional data, or results from any specific health system. They are informed by common data patterns encountered in EHR/RWD work, but they do not represent any actual patient, clinical case, or health system.
Scope. My research background is primarily in older adult populations, neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer’s disease, Parkinson’s disease, and dementia, and sleep disorders. Many examples in this blog therefore use those clinical areas. The general data-quality principles may apply broadly, but the specific examples may not generalize to all clinical populations, such as pediatric populations.

title: Generate synthetic EHR data
text: This script generates synthetic EHR-style data for practicing cohort feasibility logic. The data are fully synthetic and are intended to mimic common relational EHR structures.

title: Generate synthetic EHR data
text:
Parameter setup
These parameters control the number of synthetic patients, demographic distributions, observation window, and death rate. Changing these values will change the size and structure of the generated synthetic cohort.

patient_n <- 100 # 100 patients 
sex_ratio <- 0.5 # 50% female, 50% male 

race_levels <- c("White", "Black", "Asian", "Other")
race_dist <- c(0.60, 0.14, 0.07, 0.19)

age_mean <- 65 # mean age of patients
age_sd <- 15 # standard deviation of age

data_start <- as.Date("2016-01-01") # Date of starting EHR data 
data_end   <- as.Date("2026-06-29") # Date of query data

death_rate <- 0.05   # 5% have recorded death date

title: Generate synthetic EHR data
text:
Generate synthenic data
This chunk creates patient IDs, birth dates, sex, race, and death dates. The final output is a patient-level table with one row per PATID.

patients <- tibble(
  PATID = str_c("P", str_pad(1:patient_n, width = 4, pad = "0")),
  
  AGE_AT_DATA_END = round(rnorm(patient_n, mean = age_mean, sd = age_sd)), 
  
  BIRTH_DATE = data_end - years(AGE_AT_DATA_END) - days(sample(0:364, patient_n, replace = TRUE)), 
  
  SEX = sample(
    c("Female", "Male"),
    size = patient_n,
    replace = TRUE, 
    prob = c(sex_ratio, 1 - sex_ratio)
  ),
  
  RACE = sample(
    race_levels, 
    size = patient_n,
    replace = TRUE, 
    prob = race_dist
  ),
  
  DEATH = rbinom(patient_n, size = 1, prob = death_rate), 
  
  DEATH_DATE = if_else(
    DEATH == 1, 
    sample(seq.Date(data_start + years(1), data_end, by = "day"),
           size = patient_n, replace = TRUE), 
    as.Date(NA)
  )
) %>%
  select(PATID, BIRTH_DATE, DEATH_DATE, SEX, RACE)

write.csv(patients, "synthetic_patients.csv", row.names = FALSE, na = "")

title: Generate synthetic EHR data
text:
Parameter setup
This chunk defines the ICD-10 code dictionary and diagnosis-generation parameters. Sleep disorder diagnoses will be used to define the index date, and Alzheimer’s disease diagnoses will be used to define prevalent versus incident outcomes.

# Diagnosis code dictionary
dx_dictionary <- tibble::tribble(
  ~ICD10,   ~ICD10_TXT,   
  # Sleep disorders
  "G47.00", "Insomnia disorder",                        
  "G47.33", "Obstructive sleep apnea",                  
  "G47.9",  "Sleep disorder, unspecified",              
  
  # Alzheimer's disease
  "G30.0",  "Alzheimer's disease with early onset",     
  "G30.1",  "Alzheimer's disease with late onset",      
  "G30.9",  "Alzheimer's disease, unspecified",         
  
  # Common comorbidities for older adults population
  "I10",    "Essential hypertension",                      
  "E78.5",  "Hyperlipidemia, unspecified",                  
  "M19.90", "Osteoarthritis, unspecified site",             
  "E11.9",  "Type 2 diabetes mellitus without complications",
  "I25.10", "Atherosclerotic heart disease of native coronary artery without angina pectoris"
                        
)

# Parameter setup
sleep_dx_rate <- 0.55       # 55% have at least one sleep disorder diagnosis
ad_dx_rate <- 0.18          # 18% have Alzheimer's disease diagnosis
prevalent_ad_rate <- 0.25   # among AD cases, 25% occur before sleep diagnosis
mean_comorbid_dx <- 2       # average number of unrelated diagnoses per patient

comorbidity_codes <- c("I10", "E78.5", "M19.90", "E11.9", "I25.10")
comorbidity_prob <- c(0.35, 0.25, 0.18, 0.15, 0.07)

title: Generate synthetic EHR data
text:
Generate synthenic data

Generate sleep disorder diagnoses
This chunk randomly selects patients with sleep disorder diagnoses and assigns each selected patient one sleep-related ICD-10 code and diagnosis date.

# Select individuals with sleep disorder diagnoses
sleep_pat <- patients %>%
  slice_sample(prop = sleep_dx_rate) %>% # Randomly select individuals
  pull(PATID)

sleep_dx <- tibble(
  PATID = sleep_pat,
  ICD10 = sample(
    c("G47.00", "G47.33", "G47.9"),
    size = length(sleep_pat),
    replace = TRUE,
    prob = c(0.40, 0.45, 0.15) # Modify probabilities for specific sleep disorder dx
  ),
  DX_DATE = sample(
    seq.Date(data_start, data_end, by = "day"),
    size = length(sleep_pat),
    replace = TRUE
  )
) %>%
  left_join(dx_dictionary, by = "ICD10") %>%
  select(PATID, ICD10, ICD10_TXT, DX_DATE)



Generate Alzheimer’s disease diagnoses
This chunk randomly selects patients with Alzheimer’s disease diagnoses. In the later feasibility workflow, Alzheimer’s disease dates will be compared with sleep disorder index dates to distinguish prevalent from incident Alzheimer’s disease.

# Select individuals with Alzheimer's disease 
ad_pat <- patients %>%
  slice_sample(prop = ad_dx_rate) %>% # Randomly select individuals
  pull(PATID)

ad_dx <- tibble(PATID = ad_pat,
                 ICD10 = sample(
                   c("G30.0", "G30.1", "G30.9"),
                   size = length(ad_pat),
                   replace = TRUE,
                   prob = c(0.20, 0.50, 0.30) # Modify probabilities for specific AD dx
                 ),
                 DX_DATE = sample(
                   seq.Date(data_start, data_end, by = "day"),
                   size = length(ad_pat),
                   replace = TRUE
                 )
) %>%
  left_join(dx_dictionary, by = "ICD10") %>%
  select(PATID, ICD10, ICD10_TXT, DX_DATE)



Generate comorbidity diagnoses
This chunk adds common chronic conditions for older adults. These records make the diagnosis table look more like real EHR data, where patients often have several comorbid diagnoses.

# Generate common comorbidity diagnosis records
other_dx <- patients %>%
  select(PATID) %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(n_comorbid_dx = rpois(1, lambda = mean_comorbid_dx)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  filter(n_comorbid_dx > 0) %>%
  tidyr::uncount(n_comorbid_dx) %>%
  mutate(
    ICD10 = sample(
      comorbidity_codes,
      size = n(),
      replace = TRUE,
      prob = comorbidity_prob
    ),
    DX_DATE = sample(
      seq.Date(data_start, data_end, by = "day"),
      size = n(),
      replace = TRUE
    )
  ) %>%
  left_join(dx_dictionary, by = "ICD10") %>%
  select(PATID, ICD10, ICD10_TXT, DX_DATE)



Combine diagnosis records
This chunk stacks sleep disorder, Alzheimer’s disease, and comorbidity records into one diagnosis table. The final dx table is intentionally many-to-one: each patient can contribute multiple diagnosis records.

dx <- bind_rows(
  sleep_dx,
  ad_dx,
  other_dx
) %>%
  arrange(PATID, DX_DATE, ICD10)

# Preview
dx %>%
  slice_head(n = 15)

# A tibble: 15 × 4
   PATID ICD10  ICD10_TXT                                             DX_DATE   
   <chr> <chr>  <chr>                                                 <date>    
 1 P0001 E78.5  Hyperlipidemia, unspecified                           2018-08-08
 2 P0001 I25.10 Atherosclerotic heart disease of native coronary art… 2023-08-09
 3 P0001 G47.9  Sleep disorder, unspecified                           2024-12-02
 4 P0001 I10    Essential hypertension                                2025-12-22
 5 P0001 G30.9  Alzheimer's disease, unspecified                      2026-01-02
 6 P0002 I10    Essential hypertension                                2017-06-05
 7 P0003 E11.9  Type 2 diabetes mellitus without complications        2019-01-21
 8 P0003 E78.5  Hyperlipidemia, unspecified                           2019-11-20
 9 P0004 G47.33 Obstructive sleep apnea                               2018-12-15
10 P0005 E78.5  Hyperlipidemia, unspecified                           2019-04-08
11 P0005 E78.5  Hyperlipidemia, unspecified                           2022-10-07
12 P0005 I10    Essential hypertension                                2024-02-11
13 P0005 I10    Essential hypertension                                2024-05-29
14 P0005 M19.90 Osteoarthritis, unspecified site                      2024-06-06
15 P0006 M19.90 Osteoarthritis, unspecified site                      2016-12-31

# Output 
write.csv(dx, "synthetic_diagnoses.csv", row.names = FALSE, na = "")

title: Generate synthetic EHR data
text:
Parameter setup
These parameters control the average number of encounters per patient and the distribution of encounter types.

mean_encounters_per_patient <- 8

encounter_type_levels <- c(
  "Outpatient",
  "Primary care",
  "Specialty care",
  "ED",
  "Inpatient"
)

encounter_type_prob <- c(0.45, 0.25, 0.15, 0.08, 0.07)

title: Generate synthetic EHR data
text:
Generate synthenic data

Generate synthetic encounter data
This chunk expands each patient into multiple encounter records, assigns encounter dates and encounter types, and removes encounters occurring after death. The final table is encounter-level, not patient-level.

encounter <- patients %>%
  select(PATID, DEATH_DATE) %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(
    encounter_n = max(1, rpois(1, lambda = mean_encounters_per_patient))
    ) %>% # Ensure at least 1 encounter
  ungroup() %>%
  tidyr::uncount(encounter_n) %>%
  group_by(PATID) %>%
  mutate(
    ENCOUNTER_DATE = sample(
      seq.Date(data_start, data_end, by = "day"),
      size = n(), 
      replace = TRUE
    ), 
    ENCOUNTER_TYPE = sample(
      encounter_type_levels,
      size = n(),
      replace = TRUE,
      prob = encounter_type_prob
  )) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  # Remove encounnters that occur after death date
  filter(is.na(DEATH_DATE) | ENCOUNTER_DATE <= DEATH_DATE) %>%
  arrange(PATID, ENCOUNTER_DATE) %>%
  mutate(
    ENCOUNTER_ID = str_c("E", str_pad(row_number(), width = 3, pad = "0"))
  )
  
# Preview
encounter %>%
  slice_head(n = 10)

# A tibble: 10 × 5
   PATID DEATH_DATE ENCOUNTER_DATE ENCOUNTER_TYPE ENCOUNTER_ID
   <chr> <date>     <date>         <chr>          <chr>       
 1 P0001 NA         2018-11-11     Specialty care E001        
 2 P0001 NA         2021-02-21     Outpatient     E002        
 3 P0001 NA         2021-07-12     ED             E003        
 4 P0001 NA         2021-10-29     Outpatient     E004        
 5 P0001 NA         2023-03-08     Outpatient     E005        
 6 P0001 NA         2023-06-10     Outpatient     E006        
 7 P0001 NA         2024-07-31     Outpatient     E007        
 8 P0001 NA         2026-01-28     Outpatient     E008        
 9 P0002 NA         2017-07-27     Inpatient      E009        
10 P0002 NA         2018-08-12     Primary care   E010        

# Output
write.csv(encounter, "synthetic_encounters.csv", row.names = FALSE, na = "")

Done! Enjoy your synthetic EHR data for cohort feasibility practice.

title: Insights in Real-World Data
text:
Duplicate Records in EHR Data






EHR









Jun 26, 2026

title: Cohort Feasibility Assessment Using Synthetic EHR Data
text:
Even before you start analyzing your data and think about model or even start grant application, we need to first check whether we have enough data to do that.
In this post, suppose that we want to test feasibilty of our thought (association between sleepd disorders and incident Alzhdimer’s disease risk). The goal of this post is to test feasibilty: how many patients meet basic cohort criteria, and what are their simple demographics?
For this example, I used three synthetic EHR tables: patients, diagnoses, and encounters. The data are fully synthetic and are intended to mimic common relational EHR structures. You can create your own using R code that I shared and found in : … (add path or repositories)
The final output will be:

A cohort attrition table
A simple demographic summary of the final feasible cohort.



First of all, we need to define our cohort. For this example, we will define our cohort as follows:

Has at least one sleep disorder diagnosis.
Index date is the first sleep disorder diagnosis date.
Age is at least 20 years old at index date.
Has no Alzheimer’s disease diagnosis before or on the index date.
Has at least 365 days of observed follow-up after index date.

This is the minimum criteria for our cohort. In practice, you may have more complex criteria, but the logic will be similar.





Code
# Libraries for this project, you can ignore as it is mostly for data manipulation and database connection in R environments. -----
library(DBI) # connect to databases and send data or queries to them.
library(RSQLite) # SQLite database driver for R
library(dplyr) # data manipulation and analysis
library(readr) # read CSV files into R

con <- dbConnect(SQLite(), ":memory:") # create an in-memory SQLite database for demonstration purposes. In practice, you would connect to your actual database.

# I save the synthetic data to CSV files for demonstration purposes. In practice, you would load your actual EHR data from your database or other sources.
patients <- read_csv("data/synthetic_patients.csv") %>%
  mutate(BIRTH_DATE = as.character(BIRTH_DATE),
         DEATH_DATE = as.character(DEATH_DATE))
diagnoses <- read_csv("data/synthetic_diagnoses.csv") %>%
  mutate(DX_DATE = as.character(DX_DATE))
encounters <- read_csv("data/synthetic_encounters.csv") %>%
  mutate(ENCOUNTER_DATE = as.character(ENCOUNTER_DATE))

dbWriteTable(con, "patients", patients, overwrite = TRUE)
dbWriteTable(con, "diagnoses", diagnoses, overwrite = TRUE)
dbWriteTable(con, "encounters", encounters, overwrite = TRUE)


This creates a temporary SQLite database in memory and loads the 3 synthetic EHR tables into it.



This project uses three synthetic EHR-style tables. The important point is that the tables do not have the same unit of analysis.









Table
Unit of analysis
Key columns
Role in feasibility test




patients
One row per patient
PATID, BIRTH_DATE, DEATH_DATE, SEX, RACE
Defines baseline demographics and death date


diagnoses
Multiple rows per patient
PATID, ICD10, ICD10_TXT, DX_DATE
Defines sleep disorder index date and Alzheimer’s disease diagnosis


encounters
Multiple rows per patient
PATID, ENCOUNTER_ID, ENCOUNTER_DATE, ENCOUNTER_TYPE
Defines EHR activity, baseline lookback, and follow-up



The three tables are linked by PATID.





erDiagram
    patients ||--o{ diagnoses : "PATID"
    patients ||--o{ encounters : "PATID"

    patients {
        string PATID
        date BIRTH_DATE
        date DEATH_DATE
        string SEX
        string RACE
    }

    diagnoses {
        string PATID
        string ICD10
        string ICD10_TXT
        date DX_DATE
    }

    encounters {
        string PATID
        string ENCOUNTER_ID
        date ENCOUNTER_DATE
        string ENCOUNTER_TYPE
    }






This structure matters because joining diagnosis or encounter records directly to the patient table can create duplicate patient rows. For feasibility assessment, the final output must return to one row per patient.
Let’s quickly check first three rows of each data structure.


Code
SELECT *
FROM patients
LIMIT 3;



3 records


PATID
BIRTH_DATE
DEATH_DATE
SEX
RACE




P0001
1952-07-21
NA
Female
White


P0002
1976-07-10
NA
Male
White


P0003
1959-01-31
NA
Female
Black







Code
SELECT *
FROM diagnoses
LIMIT 3;



3 records








PATID
ICD10
ICD10_TXT
DX_DATE




P0001
E78.5
Hyperlipidemia, unspecified
2018-08-08


P0001
I25.10
Atherosclerotic heart disease of native coronary artery without angina pectoris
2023-08-09


P0001
G47.9
Sleep disorder, unspecified
2024-12-02







Code
SELECT *
FROM encounters
LIMIT 3;



3 records


PATID
ENCOUNTER_DATE
ENCOUNTER_TYPE
ENCOUNTER_ID




P0001
2016-04-09
Primary care
E001


P0001
2018-12-23
Outpatient
E002


P0001
2020-03-08
Outpatient
E003








Before defining the cohort, first thing to do is check the basic size of each table (number of unique patients)


Code
SELECT COUNT(*) AS n_pat_in_dx
FROM patients;


SELECT COUNT(*) AS n_pat_in_dx
       COUNT(DISTINCT PATID) AS n_pat_in_dx
FROM diagnoses;

SELECT COUNT(*) AS n_pat_in_encounter
       COUNT(DISTINCT PATID) AS n_pat_in_encounter
FROM encounters



1 records


n_pat_in_dx




100

title: Cohort Feasibility Assessment Using Synthetic EHR Data
text:
First of all, we need to define our cohort. For this example, we will define our cohort as follows:

Has at least one sleep disorder diagnosis.
Index date is the first sleep disorder diagnosis date.
Age is at least 20 years old at index date.
Has no Alzheimer’s disease diagnosis before or on the index date.
Has at least 365 days of observed follow-up after index date.

This is the minimum criteria for our cohort. In practice, you may have more complex criteria, but the logic will be similar.

title: Cohort Feasibility Assessment Using Synthetic EHR Data
text:
Code
# Libraries for this project, you can ignore as it is mostly for data manipulation and database connection in R environments. -----
library(DBI) # connect to databases and send data or queries to them.
library(RSQLite) # SQLite database driver for R
library(dplyr) # data manipulation and analysis
library(readr) # read CSV files into R

con <- dbConnect(SQLite(), ":memory:") # create an in-memory SQLite database for demonstration purposes. In practice, you would connect to your actual database.

# I save the synthetic data to CSV files for demonstration purposes. In practice, you would load your actual EHR data from your database or other sources.
patients <- read_csv("data/synthetic_patients.csv") %>%
  mutate(BIRTH_DATE = as.character(BIRTH_DATE),
         DEATH_DATE = as.character(DEATH_DATE))
diagnoses <- read_csv("data/synthetic_diagnoses.csv") %>%
  mutate(DX_DATE = as.character(DX_DATE))
encounters <- read_csv("data/synthetic_encounters.csv") %>%
  mutate(ENCOUNTER_DATE = as.character(ENCOUNTER_DATE))

dbWriteTable(con, "patients", patients, overwrite = TRUE)
dbWriteTable(con, "diagnoses", diagnoses, overwrite = TRUE)
dbWriteTable(con, "encounters", encounters, overwrite = TRUE)


This creates a temporary SQLite database in memory and loads the 3 synthetic EHR tables into it.

Before defining the cohort, first thing to do is check the basic size of each table (number of unique patients)
